---
title: State Management
description: Store and retrieve persistent state in Virtual Objects and Workflows
---

State management helpers are available in Virtual Object and Workflow functions for persistent key-value storage. These helpers provide strong consistency guarantees and atomic operations.

## ctx.get()

Retrieve stored state by key. State is loaded from durable storage when your function starts, so `get()` calls are fast - they read from memory, not from disk.

<CodeGroup>
```typescript TypeScript
// Get with type and default value
const profile = await ctx.get<UserProfile>("profile");
const count = (await ctx.get<number>("count")) ?? 0;
const settings = await ctx.get<Settings>("settings") ?? defaultSettings;

// Check if state exists
const lastLogin = await ctx.get<string>("lastLogin");
if (lastLogin) {
  console.log(`User last logged in at: ${lastLogin}`);
}
```

```java Java
// Get with optional handling
UserProfile profile = ctx.get("profile", UserProfile.class);
Integer count = ctx.get("count", Integer.class).orElse(0);
Settings settings = ctx.get("settings", Settings.class).orElse(defaultSettings);

// Check if state exists
Optional<String> lastLogin = ctx.get("lastLogin", String.class);
if (lastLogin.isPresent()) {
    System.out.println("User last logged in at: " + lastLogin.get());
}
```

```python Python
# Get with default values
profile = await ctx.get("profile", UserProfile)
count = await ctx.get("count", int) or 0
settings = await ctx.get("settings", Settings) or default_settings

# Check if state exists
last_login = await ctx.get("lastLogin", str)
if last_login:
    print(f"User last logged in at: {last_login}")
```
</CodeGroup>

**Key characteristics:**
- Returns `null`/`None` if the key doesn't exist
- State keys are strings
- Values can be any JSON-serializable data
- Multiple functions can read the same state simultaneously

## ctx.set()

Store state that persists across function invocations. State changes are automatically saved when your function completes successfully.

<CodeGroup>
```typescript TypeScript
// Store simple values
ctx.set("lastLogin", new Date().toISOString());
ctx.set("loginCount", loginCount + 1);

// Store complex objects
ctx.set("profile", {
  name: "John Doe",
  email: "john@example.com",
  preferences: { 
    theme: "dark",
    notifications: true 
  }
});

// Store arrays
ctx.set("favoriteItems", ["item1", "item2", "item3"]);
```

```java Java
// Store values
ctx.set("lastLogin", Instant.now().toString());
ctx.set("loginCount", loginCount + 1);
ctx.set("profile", userProfile);
ctx.set("favoriteItems", Arrays.asList("item1", "item2", "item3"));
```

```python Python
# Store values
await ctx.set("lastLogin", datetime.now().isoformat())
await ctx.set("loginCount", login_count + 1)
await ctx.set("profile", user_profile)
await ctx.set("favoriteItems", ["item1", "item2", "item3"])
```
</CodeGroup>

**Key characteristics:**
- State is only saved when the function completes successfully
- If a function fails, all state changes are rolled back
- State changes are atomic - either all changes are saved, or none are
- Only one function can modify state for a Virtual Object at a time

## ctx.clear()

Remove state keys completely. After clearing, attempts to get that key will return `null`.

<CodeGroup>
```typescript TypeScript
// Clear specific keys
ctx.clear("tempData");
ctx.clear("sessionToken");

// Clear multiple related keys
const keysToRemove = ["tempData", "processingStatus", "intermediateResults"];
keysToRemove.forEach(key => ctx.clear(key));
```

```java Java
// Clear specific keys
ctx.clear("tempData");
ctx.clear("sessionToken");

// Clear multiple keys
Arrays.asList("tempData", "processingStatus", "intermediateResults")
    .forEach(ctx::clear);
```

```python Python
# Clear specific keys
await ctx.clear("tempData")
await ctx.clear("sessionToken")

# Clear multiple keys
keys_to_remove = ["tempData", "processingStatus", "intermediateResults"]
for key in keys_to_remove:
    await ctx.clear(key)
```
</CodeGroup>

**Why clear instead of setting to null:**
- Clearing completely removes the key, saving storage space
- It's clearer in your code that you don't want this data anymore
- Some operations might treat `null` values differently than missing keys

## State consistency guarantees

Restate provides strong consistency guarantees for state management:

**Single-writer guarantee**: Only one function can modify a Virtual Object's state at any given time, preventing race conditions.

**Atomic commits**: All state changes in a function are applied together when the function completes successfully. If the function fails, all changes are rolled back.

**Isolation**: Each Virtual Object's state is completely separate from every other object's state.

**Durability**: Once saved, state survives service crashes, restarts, and infrastructure failures.

## Common patterns

### Counter pattern
Perfect for tracking counts, likes, or any numeric value:

<CodeGroup>
```typescript TypeScript
export const incrementCounter = async (ctx: ObjectContext, increment: number = 1) => {
  const currentCount = (await ctx.get<number>("count")) ?? 0;
  const newCount = currentCount + increment;
  ctx.set("count", newCount);
  
  return { count: newCount, previous: currentCount };
};
```
</CodeGroup>

### Shopping cart pattern  
Manages collections of items that change over time:

<CodeGroup>
```typescript TypeScript
export const addToCart = async (ctx: ObjectContext, item: CartItem) => {
  const items = (await ctx.get<CartItem[]>("items")) ?? [];
  items.push(item);
  
  ctx.set("items", items);
  ctx.set("lastUpdated", new Date().toISOString());
  ctx.set("itemCount", items.length);
  
  return { items, itemCount: items.length };
};
```
</CodeGroup>

### User session pattern
Validates and manages time-sensitive data:

<CodeGroup>
```typescript TypeScript
export const validateSession = async (ctx: ObjectContext) => {
  const session = await ctx.get<Session>("session");
  
  if (!session) {
    throw new Error("No active session");
  }
  
  if (session.expires < Date.now()) {
    ctx.clear("session");
    throw new Error("Session expired");
  }
  
  // Update last accessed time
  session.lastAccessed = Date.now();
  ctx.set("session", session);
  
  return session;
};
```
</CodeGroup>

### Configuration pattern
Store and update application settings:

<CodeGroup>
```typescript TypeScript
export const updateSettings = async (ctx: ObjectContext, newSettings: Partial<Settings>) => {
  const currentSettings = await ctx.get<Settings>("settings") ?? defaultSettings;
  
  const updatedSettings = {
    ...currentSettings,
    ...newSettings,
    lastModified: new Date().toISOString()
  };
  
  ctx.set("settings", updatedSettings);
  ctx.set("version", ((await ctx.get<number>("version")) ?? 0) + 1);
  
  return updatedSettings;
};
```
</CodeGroup>

State management helpers provide reliable, consistent data storage for your Virtual Objects and Workflows without requiring external databases or complex coordination mechanisms.
